ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No precise SNOMED CT code identified for packaged flavoured yoghurt drink; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged flavoured yoghurt drink in Singapore is approximately 200 ml (200 g), equivalent to one small bottle or carton.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured Yoghurt Drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for packaged flavoured yoghurt drink (cr_id D02020003). All per-100g macros, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for packaged flavoured yoghurt drink in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 36 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: low-fat flavoured yoghurt (GI ~33–36, University of Sydney GI Database). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (36) × available carbohydrate per serving (21.14 g from 200 g serving) ÷ 100 = 7.6. This is consistent with the low-GI classification of fermented dairy products with added sugar.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.43 g/100 g) in a beverage format exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold for hypertension risk; sodium is low at 41 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened yoghurt drink; the 9.43 g sugar/100 g in liquid form independently raises blood pressure risk (AHA 2018) despite the low sodium content of 41 mg/100 g and beneficial calcium of 153 mg/100 g — choose plain unsweetened kefir or yoghurt drink instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.43 g sugar/100 g and only 0.36 g dietary fibre/100 g; liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed causing acute postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this flavoured yoghurt drink because the 9.43 g sugar/100 g in beverage form with minimal fibre (0.36 g/100 g) causes rapid glucose absorption and postprandial hyperglycaemia; plain unsweetened yoghurt with separately added fibre-rich fruit is the preferred alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar content (9.43 g/100 g) in a beverage raises cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation and endothelial dysfunction; saturated fat is low (~0.6 g/100 g) and sodium is low (41 mg/100 g).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high added sugar load (9.43 g/100 g) in this beverage is the primary concern as regular consumption of sugar-sweetened dairy drinks is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk; the low saturated fat (~0.6 g/100 g) and low sodium (41 mg/100 g) are favourable but do not offset the sugar-driven risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fermented dairy with live cultures supports gut-immune modulation and calcium (153 mg/100 g) protects bone in corticosteroid-treated RA patients, but 9.43 g sugar/100 g may promote pro-inflammatory mediators.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the live cultures (Lactobacillus, Streptococcus thermophilus) and calcium (153 mg/100 g) in this yoghurt drink, but the 9.43 g sugar/100 g can promote inflammatory mediators — plain unsweetened yoghurt drink is strongly preferred; confirm live cultures are present on the label and not heat-treated post-fermentation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured yoghurt drink (packaged, any flavour) is a ready-to-drink fermented dairy beverage widely available in Singapore supermarkets and convenience stores. It is produced by fermenting skimmed or low-fat milk with live yoghurt cultures (typically Lactobacillus bulgaricus and Streptococcus thermophilus), then blending with added sugar and natural or artificial fruit flavourings such as strawberry, peach, lychee, or mango. The texture is smooth and pourable — thinner than spoonable yoghurt — with a mildly tangy, sweet flavour profile. Per 100 g, it provides 57 kcal, 1.86 g protein, 0.9 g fat (approximately 0.6 g saturated), 10.57 g carbohydrate (9.43 g sugar), 0.36 g dietary fibre, 41 mg sodium, 153 mg calcium, and 0.55 mg iron. The high calcium content (153 mg/100 g) is nutritionally significant for bone health. Clinically, this product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its 9.43 g sugar/100 g in liquid form; it is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension. The low GI (~36) reflects the fermented dairy matrix but does not negate the rapid glucose impact of liquid sugar with minimal fibre. It is a common snack item in Singapore, often consumed by children and adults as a portable dairy snack. Halal-certified versions are widely available. The product is vegetarian but not vegan.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in a yoghurt drink?
Is drinking yoghurt good for blood pressure?
Calories in packaged yoghurt drink Singapore
Is yoghurt drink suitable for hypertension?
Yoghurt drink vs plain yoghurt nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D02020003) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from typical low-fat yoghurt drink composition (~0.6 g/100 g) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using low-fat flavoured yoghurt as the closest match.
notes: Saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for low-fat flavoured yoghurt drink (closest analogue) as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this record. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this packaged flavoured yoghurt drink are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific product category as of 2026. GI of 36 is an analogous estimate based on low-fat flavoured yoghurt from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this exact product. This food is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) for clinical purposes due to its 9.43 g sugar/100 g in liquid form. Halal status is typical for Singapore-market products but should be verified on individual product labels. dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input record are preserved as contextually accurate (sodium 41 mg/100 g; energy 57 kcal/100 g).
